content.problemStatement:
Chef recently discovered a function XOR()XOR()XOR(), which computes the XOR of all elements of a sequence:
XOR(a1..n)=a1⊕a2⊕⋯⊕an .XOR(a_{1..n}) = a_1 \oplus a_2 \oplus \dots \oplus a_n\,.XOR(a1..n​)=a1​⊕a2​⊕⋯⊕an​.

Chef has a sequence AAA with size NNN. He generated a new sequence BBB with size N2N^2N2 using the following formula:
BiN+j+1=(Ai+1+Aj+1)∀  0≤i,j<N .B_{iN+j+1} = (A_{i+1} + A_{j+1}) \quad \forall\; 0 \le i, j \lt N\,.BiN+j+1​=(Ai+1​+Aj+1​)∀0≤i,j<N.

Compute the value of XOR(B)XOR(B)XOR(B).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the size of array A. The next line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the XOR of all N² elements of sequence B, where B_{i·N+j+1}=A_{i+1}+A_{j+1}.

sampleTestCases.explanation: XOR of A is 1⊕2 = 3. The answer equals 3 shifted left by one, i.e., 6.
XOR of A is 5. Answer = 5 << 1 = 10, which equals the only element B1 = 5+5.

solution.approach: For every ordered pair (i, j) we have B_{i·N+j+1}=A_i+A_j. All off‑diagonal pairs (i≠j) appear twice with the same value, so their contributions cancel out in the XOR. Only the N diagonal elements (i=j) remain, each equal to 2·A_i. Hence the required XOR equals XOR_{i=1..N}(2·A_i) = (XOR_{i=1..N} A_i) << 1. The algorithm is therefore: compute xr = XOR of all A_i, output xr shifted left by one. This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        xr = 0
        for _ in range(n):
            xr ^= int(next(it))
        out.append(str(xr << 1))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
